## [00:00 - 03:01]

Listen, in this session, I'd like to talk to you about how to design a course or how to design a talk that will influence. Because when I'm doing a program, I'm not just a program to just tell somebody something or teach them intellectually, because you can learn things and then your learning leads to knowledge and you become a fool. If your learning leads to action, as my friend Jim Rohn used to say, then you can do anything you want with your life. And so almost all the programs that I do, I'm thinking in terms of how do I influence someone to improve the quality of their life? And I think about what it is that I'm most passionate about that I want to communicate. But I'd like you to think about the structure a little bit. And I'm not expecting you to do this perfectly. And I want to remind you that the two most important pieces of growing your business is to understand that everything is innovation and marketing, right? We've talked about this. And if you're going to innovate, that really means you're going to find a better way to meet the needs of your client. And marketing is getting the want to do business with you. Well, when you're designing a talk, you still want to make sure that the talk itself is going to develop or the seminar, the mastermind, whatever you're creating, that it's going to meet the needs of the individual people you're talking to this time. So it doesn't matter if I'm doing a 20,000 person event or I'm doing a, you know, maybe five, 6,000 person event for four hours, but all the audiences I talked to were completely different, different demographics, psychographics. And today now I work in a hundred countries. So you couldn't have more different cultures and I got to serve them all. So how do I do that? Whether it's a corporate culture or it's one of my own seminars, if it's in Italy or Germany or Singapore, wherever the case may be Abu Dhabi, I start with something called Bend Wimp, B-E-M-D-W-I-M-P. And all it is is an acronym. And I think this is a useful thing. I want to know who my audience is. So think about this. If you're going to be successful in business, you have to know who's your ideal client. Like if you want to influence somebody, you got to know what already influences them. I'll say it again, to influence another human being, you got to know what already influences them. And everyone's different. We're all influenced by our emotions. We're all influenced by the state ran. We're all influenced by our blueprints, our way of looking at life. And so I try to figure out a little bit about that audience. So even when I do, like I said, a 5,000 person mega event, it's only three or four hours, I send my team into the audience in advance earlier in the day or the night before, and I have an interview at least 20 people. And I ask them a whole, I'm asking a series of questions and they send me that poor and I read it so that my mind is geared up so I can gear what I'm talking about, even if I have a certain subject matter, I know I want to make it precisely to meet their needs. And that's how you over deliver. So what has been what stand for real simple? First is I want to know what do they believe? What are the things that are most important to them? ## [03:01 - 06:01]

Like, how do they make decisions about what things mean? What are they going to compare me to? So if I'm talking to, let's say, I'll give an example at the end of a fun story. I'll say an NBA team. Um, and I'm this white guy walking in who, you know, doesn't play professional basketball. You know, what's the first thing that's about me is why is, why should I give this guy my time? Why am I sitting here talking to a guy that doesn't play basketball? And I got to be able to answer that question. What do they want? Cause, you know, people want their desires met, but sometimes you meet your desires, you don't meet your needs. And my job is really over deliver. If you can talk about something that they're hurting about, you're going to pull their interest in, not if you make them wrong for it. But if you just say, look, I've had this problem. So if I'm doing a corporate training and I got 5, 10,000 people, 3,000 people, whatever's for big corporation, and they bring me in, and let's say it's a, you know, it's a training for all their top marketing or sales people. I will go in and I will interview people, but I'll also ask, I want to talk to your top three or top five salespeople's marketers, whatever the top people are in that group. people are proud about and you can connect to them that way, it bonds you to them. So this is kind of just a general format that I try to do.
